mappings.rationaleAI: Megaliths served as burial monuments, ritual gathering sites, and possible locations for offerings across Neolithic and Bronze Age societies, directly illuminating inferred prehistoric religious beliefs (A.2), burial customs and cults of the dead (A.2.a), sacrificial and ritual offerings (A.2.c), and religions attributed to various prehistoric cultural stages and regions (A.3).

Stone Age cave paintings of prey animals and abstract symbols at sites like Chauvet and Lascaux raise questions about hunting rites and animal cults (A.2.d), possible shamanic or magical purposes (A.2.f), and the interpretive methods scholars use to reconstruct prehistoric religious expression (A.1).

Aztec religious cosmology and institutionalized human sacrifice exemplify indigenous religious practices of pre-Columbian Mesoamerica (B.5.c), with the centrality of sacrificial ritual connecting directly to sacrifice and purification rites in primitive religion (B.4.a).

Dragon mythology across cultures reflects animistic views of external reality as living presence (B.2.c), and the nature and function of myth and symbol in primitive religion including their iconographic character (B.3).

Fairy beliefs reflect animistic views of external reality as living presence (B.2.c), sacred conceptions of man's nature and destiny including cosmologies about loss and the supernatural (B.2.d), and the function of myth and symbol in religion (B.3).
